section.title: David's Census and the Threshing Floor
section.sectionRationale: After a whole chapter of victories that the LORD gave, David falls into a subtler sin, numbering his fighting men, a trust in numbers rather than in the God who had just given him victory wherever he went. Judgment falls, David repents, and mercy meets him at the threshing floor of Ornan the Jebusite, the very ground David buys, builds an altar on, and which the Chronicler later tells us becomes the site of Solomon's temple. For the exiles, this chapter is the origin story of the ground beneath their rebuilt temple: a place marked forever by sin exposed, judgment stayed, and costly mercy.
section.thesis: David's pride in numbering Israel brings real judgment, but at the very point of Jerusalem's destruction the LORD relents, and the threshing floor where the destroying angel is stayed becomes, through costly sacrifice and a price fully paid, the appointed site of God's future house, so that mercy, not judgment, has the last word over the ground where Israel will worship forever.
section.frameNote: Five beats: David's census, incited by Satan, verses 1 to 6; judgment announced and David's confession, verses 7 to 13; the plague and the destroying angel stayed at Ornan's threshing floor, verses 14 to 17; David buys the site and builds an altar, verses 18 to 27; and why David sacrificed there rather than at Gibeon, verses 28 to 30.

units.label: David's census, incited by Satan
units.note: why should he bring guilt on Israel

verses.text: Then {{satan|Satan}} rose up against Israel and incited David to take a census of Israel.
So David said to Joab and the commanders of the troops, "Go and count the Israelites from Beersheba to Dan and bring me a report, so that I may {{manah|know their number}}."

But Joab replied, "May the LORD multiply His troops a hundred times over. My lord the king, are they not all servants of my lord? Why does my lord want to do this? Why should he bring guilt on Israel?"
Nevertheless, the king's word prevailed against Joab. So Joab departed and traveled throughout Israel, and then he returned to Jerusalem.
And Joab reported to David the total number of the troops. In all Israel there were 1,100,000 men who drew the sword, including 470,000 in Judah.
But Joab did not include Levi and Benjamin in the count, because the king's command was detestable to him.
This command was also evil in the sight of God; so He struck Israel.
Then David said to God, "I have sinned greatly because I have done this thing. Now I beg You to take away the iniquity of Your servant, for I have acted very foolishly."
And the LORD instructed Gad, David's seer,
"Go and tell David that this is what the LORD says: 'I am offering you three options. Choose one of them, and I will carry it out against you.'"
So Gad went and said to David, "This is what the LORD says: 'You must choose

between three years of famine, three months of being swept away before your enemies and overtaken by their swords, or three days of the sword of the LORD, days of plague upon the land, with the angel of the LORD ravaging every part of Israel.' Now then, decide how I should reply to Him who sent me."
David answered Gad, "I am deeply distressed. Please, let me fall into the hand of the LORD, for His mercies are very great; but do not let me fall into the hands of men."

units.label: The plague, and the destroying angel stayed

verses.text: So the LORD sent a plague upon Israel, and seventy thousand men of Israel fell dead.

Then God sent an angel to destroy Jerusalem, but as the angel was doing so, the LORD saw it and relented from the calamity, and He said to the angel who was {{mashchit|destroying}} the people, "Enough! Withdraw your hand now!" At that time the angel of the LORD was standing by the threshing floor of Ornan the Jebusite.

When David lifted up his eyes and saw the angel of the LORD standing between heaven and earth, with a drawn sword in his hand stretched out over Jerusalem, David and the elders, clothed in sackcloth, fell facedown.

And David said to God, "Was it not I who gave the order to count the people? I am the one who has sinned and acted wickedly. But these sheep, what have they done? O LORD my God, please let Your hand fall upon me and my father's house, but do not let this plague remain upon Your people."

units.label: David buys the threshing floor
units.note: the full price, not what costs me nothing

verses.text: Then the angel of the LORD ordered Gad to tell David to go up and build an altar to the LORD on the {{goren|threshing floor}} of Ornan the Jebusite.
So David went up at the word that Gad had spoken in the name of the LORD.
Now Ornan was threshing wheat when he turned and saw the angel; and his four sons who were with him hid themselves.
David came to Ornan, and when Ornan looked out and saw David, he left the threshing floor and bowed facedown before David.
Then David said to Ornan, "Grant me the site of this threshing floor, that I may build an altar to the LORD. Sell it to me for the full price, so that the plague upon the people may be halted."

Ornan said to David, "Take it! May my lord the king do whatever seems good to him. Look, I will give the oxen for the burnt offerings, the threshing sledges for the wood, and the wheat for the grain offering, I will give it all."
"No," replied King David, "I insist on paying the full price, for I will not take for the LORD what belongs to you, nor will I offer burnt offerings that cost me nothing."
So David paid Ornan six hundred shekels of gold for the site.
And there he built an altar to the LORD and offered burnt offerings and peace offerings. He called upon the LORD, who answered him with fire from heaven on the altar of burnt offering.
Then the LORD spoke to the angel, who put his sword back into its sheath.

units.label: Why David sacrificed there
units.note: afraid of the sword of the angel

verses.text: At that time, when David saw that the LORD had answered him at the threshing floor of Ornan the Jebusite, he offered sacrifices there.
For the tabernacle of the LORD that Moses had made in the wilderness and the altar of burnt offering were presently at the high place in Gibeon,
but David could not go before it to inquire of God, because he was afraid of the sword of the angel of the LORD.

satan.gloss: Adversary, accuser. This is one of the clearest Old Testament uses of satan as a proper name for a personal, hostile spiritual being, incitement the parallel account in 2 Samuel 24:1 describes from another angle as the LORD's anger moving David to number Israel. Both truths stand together: a real adversary tempted David, under God's sovereign permission, and David remained fully responsible for his own sin.

manah.gloss: To count, number, or appoint. David's command to 'know their number' uses this root; the sin was not counting itself, which Moses also did, but the prideful trust in military numbers rather than in the LORD who had just given David victory wherever he went, 1 Chronicles 18:6, 13.

mashchit.gloss: The destroyer, or the one destroying. The angel of the LORD is called the mashchit as he ravages Israel with plague, the same word used of the destroying angel in the Passover account, Exodus 12:23, until the LORD says 'Enough' and stays his hand at Ornan's threshing floor.

goren.gloss: Threshing floor, an open, elevated area where grain was separated from chaff. Ornan's goren, where judgment is halted and an altar is built, becomes, according to 2 Chronicles 3:1, the very site of Solomon's temple, forever marking Israel's place of worship as ground where mercy triumphed over deserved judgment.

basic.context: Fresh off a whole chapter of God given victories, David commands a military census of Israel, wanting to know the number of his fighting men, an act the text says Satan incited and that even hardened Joab recognized as bringing guilt on Israel. God is displeased and offers David a choice of three judgments through the prophet Gad: three years of famine, three months of fleeing before enemies, or three days of plague. David chooses to fall into God's hands rather than man's, trusting God's mercy. Seventy thousand die in the plague, and as the destroying angel reaches Jerusalem itself, the LORD relents and commands, 'Enough!' The angel is seen standing at the threshing floor of Ornan the Jebusite. David and the elders, in sackcloth, plead for the plague to fall on David alone rather than the innocent people. Told to build an altar there, David refuses Ornan's generous offer to give the site and materials for free, insisting on paying full price, because he will not offer to the LORD what costs him nothing. Fire falls from heaven, the plague stops, and this very threshing floor becomes, as 2 Chronicles 3:1 tells us, the site of Solomon's temple. Key questions: why was numbering the people sinful? How can both Satan and God's anger be said to have incited David? And why does costly, willing sacrifice matter so much to David and to God?
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative recording a real national sin, a real plague, and a real, specific location that later becomes central to Israel's worship. It describes what happened rather than prescribing census taking as inherently wrong, since Moses himself numbered Israel under God's command, Numbers 1. The sin here was pride and self reliant trust in military strength rather than in the LORD, following immediately after a chapter that showed God alone gives victory. The chapter also carefully holds together God's sovereignty and Satan's real agency without erasing David's full responsibility.
basic.originalLanguages: The chapter opens with satan, the adversary, inciting David, while 2 Samuel 24:1 describes the same event from the angle of the LORD's own anger, both true at once. David's sin was to manah, count or number, his troops in prideful reliance on strength rather than on the LORD. The angel sent is called the mashchit, the destroyer, the same word used of the angel who passed through Egypt at the Passover, until God says enough. And it all ends at the goren, the threshing floor, of Ornan the Jebusite, ground that becomes Solomon's temple site.
basic.typology: The threshing floor where judgment is stayed and an altar is raised sits, according to 2 Chronicles 3:1, on Mount Moriah, the same region where Abraham once offered Isaac and found the LORD would provide, Genesis 22:2, 14. David's insistence on paying the full price, refusing a sacrifice that costs him nothing, foreshadows the costly, once for all sacrifice of Christ, who alone can truly halt the destroyer and avert God's wrath from His people.
basic.christ: David's plea, 'let Your hand fall upon me and my father's house, but do not let this plague remain upon Your people,' is the cry of a shepherd king willing to bear judgment for his flock, a cry answered not in David himself but in great David's greater Son, who actually did bear the judgment His people deserved. The mashchit, the destroyer, is halted here by mercy alone; at the cross the destroyer is finally and fully answered when Christ, the Passover Lamb, absorbs the judgment in Himself. And the ground of the averted plague becomes the ground of the temple, anticipating Christ, our true meeting place with God.
basic.god: God is holy, and sin, even the quieter sin of pride in numbers and strength, brings real consequences. Yet God is also the God who relents, who says 'Enough' before Jerusalem is destroyed, and who receives a costly, willing sacrifice and answers with fire from heaven. The whole scene shows a God whose justice and mercy are not at odds but meet exactly at the place where judgment is turned aside.
basic.crossReferences: 2 Samuel 24 gives the parallel account, attributing the incitement to the LORD's anger rather than Satan. Exodus 30:11-16 required an atonement payment whenever Israel's men were numbered, so that a census without ransom money brought guilt, the very danger this chapter illustrates. 2 Chronicles 3:1 identifies this threshing floor as the site of Solomon's temple on Mount Moriah. 2 Samuel 24:16-17 mirrors David's plea to bear judgment himself rather than his people.
basic.oneStory: David's pride brings judgment, but at the point of Jerusalem's destruction, mercy triumphs, and the very ground where the destroyer was stayed becomes, generations later, the site of God's own house. The whole scene, sin exposed, judgment threatened, costly sacrifice offered, and mercy secured, is a small preview of the gospel, fulfilled at the cross where Christ, the far costlier sacrifice, forever turns aside the wrath we deserved.

context.literary: The chapter turns on a series of contrasts: pride and confession, threatened destruction and stayed judgment, a free offer refused and a full price insisted upon. It is the hinge between the war narratives of chapters 18 to 20 and the temple preparation material of chapters 22 to 29, since this very site becomes, in the next chapter, 'the house of the LORD God' David designates for Solomon, 22:1.
context.historical: A threshing floor was typically an open, elevated, wind swept area outside a city, ideal for separating grain from chaff, and often served as neutral or commonly used ground, explaining why Ornan the Jebusite, a non-Israelite resident, still owned this plot near Jerusalem. Six hundred shekels of gold, roughly 15 pounds, was a very large sum, showing that David paid an intentionally costly, generous price rather than a token amount, consistent with his stated principle in verse 24.
context.canonical: This chapter is the origin story of the temple mount. 2 Chronicles 3:1 explicitly identifies the site as Mount Moriah, the same region where Abraham, centuries earlier, offered Isaac and discovered that the LORD would provide a substitute, Genesis 22:2, 14. For the exiles rebuilding the temple, knowing that its very ground was marked by both deserved judgment and undeserved mercy would have been a source of profound hope and humility.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that David's pride in numbering Israel's fighting strength brought real, national judgment, and to trace, in careful detail, how God's mercy triumphed over that judgment at a specific, nameable place, the threshing floor that would become the site of His own future house, grounding the exiles' hope in the very ground beneath their rebuilt temple.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter narrates a specific historical sin and its consequences; it does not prescribe that all census taking is sinful, since Moses numbered Israel under direct divine command, Numbers 1:1-3. What it does teach for every reader is the danger of pride and self reliance, the reality of God's mercy toward genuine repentance, and the principle that true worship costs the worshiper something.

debates.question: Who incited David to take the census, Satan or the LORD?

views.label: Both are true, at different levels of causation
views.case: 1 Chronicles 21:1 says Satan incited David, while 2 Samuel 24:1 says the LORD's anger against Israel moved David to number the people; the two are not contradictory but complementary, much as in Job 1 to 2, where Satan acts as the immediate tempter while remaining fully under God's sovereign permission and purpose, here perhaps as a form of judgment on Israel more broadly, verse 7 confirming God was also displeased with what followed. David remains fully and personally responsible for his own sin, verse 8.

views.label: The Chronicler reflects a developed understanding of Satan's personal agency
views.case: Others note that 1 Chronicles, written centuries after 2 Samuel, may reflect Israel's clearer, more developed theology of a personal adversary named Satan, naming explicitly what 2 Samuel described only in terms of divine providence; this does not contradict 2 Samuel but articulates the spiritual reality behind it more explicitly for a later generation.

debates.bearing: Either way, Scripture consistently holds together real, personal spiritual opposition and God's sovereign hand, without ever excusing David's own sin; the burden of guilt and the need for repentance remain entirely David's own, verses 8 and 17.

debates.question: What exactly made numbering the fighting men sinful, since Moses also took censuses under God's command?

views.label: Pride and misplaced trust in military strength
views.case: Coming right after a chapter celebrating that 'the LORD made David victorious wherever he went,' 18:6, 13, this census reflects a shift from trusting God's proven faithfulness to trusting the visible strength of a standing army, a subtle but serious form of pride that even Joab recognized as bringing guilt on Israel, verse 3.

views.label: A violation of the required atonement money of Exodus 30
views.case: Exodus 30:11-16 required every man counted in a military census to pay a half shekel ransom for his life 'so that no plague will come upon them when you number them'; the text of 1 Chronicles 21 never mentions this atonement payment being collected, suggesting the census itself, apart from the prescribed ransom, exposed Israel to exactly the plague that then came.

debates.bearing: Both views agree the underlying issue was a failure to trust and honor the LORD properly in the exercise of counting military strength, whether through prideful self reliance or through neglecting the atonement God had required; either way, the mercy that follows shows God's discipline is never separated from His covenant faithfulness.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter opens with satan, the adversary, rising against Israel to incite David, a name and role Scripture elsewhere develops into the personal, hostile accuser confronted fully in Christ's ministry. David's sin was to manah, count or number, his fighting men, trusting strength rather than the LORD's proven faithfulness. Judgment comes through the mashchit, the destroyer, the same title given the angel of the Passover in Egypt, until the LORD says 'Enough' and the destroyer is stayed at Ornan's goren, threshing floor, ground that becomes, by God's own design, the site of His future house.

typology.type: The destroying angel stayed by mercy at the threshing floor
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the true and better sacrifice, who fully and finally turns aside God's wrath from His people, Romans 5:9.

typology.type: David refusing a sacrifice that costs him nothing
typology.fulfillment: The costly, once for all sacrifice of Christ, who gave not what was easy but His very life, Hebrews 9:26.

typology.type: The threshing floor of Ornan, on Mount Moriah, becoming the temple site
typology.fulfillment: The place where God provides a substitute, echoing Abraham on that same mountain, Genesis 22:14, and anticipating Christ, our true meeting place with God, John 2:19-21.

study.christ: David's anguished plea, 'let Your hand fall upon me and my father's house, but do not let this plague remain upon Your people,' is the cry of a shepherd king willing, in principle, to bear judgment in his people's place, though he himself could not actually do so. That longing finds its true answer in Jesus, the greater Son of David, who did bear in His own body the judgment His people deserved, 1 Peter 2:24. David's refusal to offer the LORD 'burnt offerings that cost me nothing' anticipates the far costlier, once for all sacrifice of Christ, who gave not a token but His very life, Hebrews 9:26. And the ground where the destroyer was stayed, on Mount Moriah, the same region where Abraham once found that the LORD would provide, Genesis 22:14, becomes Solomon's temple site, 2 Chronicles 3:1, foreshadowing Christ, in whom God and His people finally and fully meet.
study.god: God is holy, and David's pride, however it was ultimately incited, brings real and devastating national consequences, seventy thousand dead. Yet God is also profoundly merciful, relenting from destroying Jerusalem itself and saying 'Enough' before the judgment is complete. God receives, and answers with fire from heaven, a costly sacrifice freely and fully paid for, showing that true worship must cost the worshiper something. In this chapter, justice and mercy are not opposed; they meet precisely at the threshing floor where judgment is turned aside.

crossReferences.note: The parallel account, attributing the incitement to the LORD's anger rather than naming Satan directly, and recording the same threshing floor purchase under the name Araunah.
The required atonement ransom whenever Israel's fighting men were numbered, so that an uncompensated census could indeed bring plague.
Abraham offers Isaac in the region of Moriah and discovers that the LORD will provide, the same region later identified as the temple site, 2 Chronicles 3:1.
Explicitly identifies the threshing floor of Ornan as Mount Moriah, the site where Solomon built the temple.

study.oneStory: David's pride in numbering his army brings real judgment on Israel, but at the very moment of Jerusalem's destruction, God relents, and the threshing floor where the destroyer is stayed becomes, through a costly, willingly paid sacrifice, the appointed site of God's own future house. Judgment exposed, mercy secured, and a costly price paid, all converge on this one plot of ground, anticipating the far greater mercy secured at the cross, where Christ, the truly costly and sufficient sacrifice, forever turns aside the judgment His people deserved.

quiz.q: What incited David to take a census of Israel's fighting men, and how does this relate to the parallel account in 2 Samuel 24?

options.text: Satan incited David, 21:1, while 2 Samuel 24:1 describes the same event through the LORD's anger against Israel, both true without contradiction.
options.note: The two texts are complementary, as in Job 1-2, where a real adversary tempts while remaining under God's sovereign purpose; David is still fully responsible, verse 8.

options.text: David simply decided on his own initiative, with no spiritual influence mentioned in either account.
options.note: Both accounts explicitly name a spiritual cause behind David's decision, Satan in Chronicles and the LORD's anger in Samuel.

options.text: Joab incited David by suggesting the census himself.
options.note: The opposite: Joab actively objected to the census and tried to dissuade David, verse 3.

options.text: Satan incited David, and this fully contradicts and cannot be reconciled with 2 Samuel 24's account.
options.note: This overstates the tension; most careful readers see the two accounts as complementary rather than flatly irreconcilable.

quiz.q: How did David respond once he realized the census was sinful?

options.text: He confessed plainly to God, 'I have sinned greatly... I have acted very foolishly,' and begged God to take away his iniquity.
options.note: Verse 8 records David's direct, humble confession.

options.text: He blamed Joab for carrying out the census.
options.note: David takes personal responsibility rather than blaming Joab, who had actually tried to stop him.

options.text: He offered a sacrifice immediately without any word of confession.
options.note: Confession, verse 8, comes before any sacrifice is offered; the altar comes later, at Ornan's threshing floor.

options.text: He asked Gad the seer what judgment God would send, then confessed afterward.
options.note: The order is reversed; David confesses first, in verse 8, and only afterward is told through Gad about the three options in verses 9-12.

quiz.q: Of the three judgments offered, what did David choose and why?

options.text: He chose to fall into the hand of the LORD rather than the hand of men, trusting that God's mercies are very great.
options.note: Verse 13 records David's explicit reasoning: he trusted God's great mercy over human cruelty.

options.text: He chose three years of famine because it seemed the mildest option.
options.note: David did not choose famine; he chose to fall into the LORD's hand through the plague option, trusting God's mercy rather than picking based on perceived mildness.

options.text: He refused to choose and let Gad decide for him.
options.note: David explicitly makes the choice himself in verse 13, trusting the LORD's hand.

options.text: He chose defeat before his enemies because it would spare civilian lives.
options.note: David actually rejected the option of fleeing before enemies; his stated reason for his actual choice was trust in God's great mercy, not calculation about which option spared the most lives.

quiz.q: Why did David insist on paying full price for Ornan's threshing floor rather than accepting it as a free gift?

options.text: Because he refused to offer the LORD burnt offerings that cost him nothing.
options.note: Verse 24 states David's principle explicitly: true sacrifice must be costly to the worshiper.

options.text: Because Ornan demanded an exorbitant price and David had no choice.
options.note: The opposite: Ornan offered everything for free, and David insisted on paying anyway.

options.text: Because Israelite law required all land purchases to be paid in gold.
options.note: No such general law is cited; David's stated reason is personal and theological, not a generic legal requirement.

options.text: Because he wanted to be generous to Ornan, who had lost the use of his threshing floor.
options.note: Generosity to Ornan may be a secondary effect, but David's own stated reason in verse 24 is about the nature of true sacrifice to the LORD, not primarily generosity to Ornan.

quiz.q: What later becomes of the threshing floor where the plague was stopped?

options.text: According to 2 Chronicles 3:1, it becomes the site of Solomon's temple on Mount Moriah.
options.note: 2 Chronicles 3:1 explicitly identifies this location as the temple site, the same region where Abraham once offered Isaac, Genesis 22:2.

options.text: It is abandoned and never used again after David's sacrifice.
options.note: The opposite: this ground becomes central to Israel's ongoing worship as the temple site.

options.text: It becomes the site of David's own palace.
options.note: David's palace was elsewhere in the city; this specific site becomes the temple, not the palace.

options.text: It remains simply Ornan's working threshing floor, unconnected to later worship.
options.note: It was indeed Ornan's threshing floor, but the text and 2 Chronicles 3:1 make clear it becomes far more, the appointed site of the temple.

quiz.q: What does the pattern of judgment stayed and costly sacrifice offered at the threshing floor point toward?

options.text: The far greater and final turning aside of God's wrath accomplished through Christ's costly, once for all sacrifice.
options.note: Romans 5:9 and Hebrews 9:26 present Christ's death as the true and sufficient answer to the wrath David's sacrifice could only temporarily and typologically address.

options.text: That God's wrath can always be avoided through any sincere religious gesture, regardless of cost.
options.note: The passage specifically emphasizes cost, David refusing what cost him nothing, undercutting the idea that any casual gesture suffices.

options.text: That national leaders bear no special responsibility before God for the sins of their people.
options.note: The opposite: David's own sin as leader brought real consequences on the whole nation, and he pleads to bear the judgment himself.

options.text: That the temple's location was chosen for practical, geographic convenience rather than any theological significance.
options.note: The location does have historical logic, but the text and 2 Chronicles 3:1 tie it to specific theological events, not mere geographic convenience.

questions: David's sin here was quiet pride, trusting numbers and strength rather than the God who had just given him victory. Where are you tempted to trust your own resources rather than God's faithfulness?
David chose to fall into God's hand rather than man's, trusting His great mercy. When you have sinned, do you tend to run from God or toward Him in honest confession?
David refused to offer God a sacrifice that cost him nothing. What would it look like for your worship, giving, or obedience this week to actually cost you something?
The ground where judgment was stayed became the site of God's own house. Where in your own story has God brought worship and hope out of a place marked by failure or judgment?
David pleaded to bear the judgment himself rather than let it fall on his people. How does knowing that Christ actually did bear your judgment change the way you receive His mercy today?

translationNotes.note: 1 Chronicles attributes the incitement to Satan, while the parallel in 2 Samuel 24:1 attributes it to the LORD's anger against Israel; both are honestly presented in this study as complementary rather than contradictory, consistent with the pattern in Job 1-2.

The troop totals here, 1,100,000 with 470,000 in Judah, and the famine option of three years, differ slightly from 2 Samuel 24:9 and 24:13, which give 800,000 and 500,000 and a seven year famine. These are recognized textual and counting differences between the two accounts, honestly noted rather than resolved with certainty; the Chronicler may be counting by different categories or using differing textual traditions.
The Hebrew name for the site's owner appears here as Ornan; the parallel in 2 Samuel 24:16 uses Araunah. Most scholars regard these as variant spellings of the same name.

textToVerify: The differing census totals and famine duration between 1 Chronicles 21:5, 12 and 2 Samuel 24:9, 13 are a recognized textual question among conservative scholars and are noted honestly in the translation notes rather than resolved with certainty.
meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 1 Chronicles 21:1-30. Two em dashes in the source, at verses 12 and 23, were converted to commas; no dashes remain in this file. A search of Alistair Begg, Tim Keller, John Piper, Sinclair Ferguson, and D.A. Carson's available sermon archives found no message preached specifically and expositionally on this chapter; the one Begg message covering this passage's range, 'The Partial Kingdom: God's King,' surveys the entire span from Judges to 2 Chronicles rather than treating 1 Chronicles 21 specifically, so sermon status is recorded as none.
